coredata.dc:description: Polyethyleneimine (PEI), playing roles as a templating agent, has been successfully applied to prepare blue-green-emitting copper nanoclusters (Cu NCs@PEI) on the basis of a rapid chemical reduction synthesis method. Some characterization methods, such as fluorescence spectrophotometer, UV spectrophotometer, Fourier transform infrared instrument (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), transmission electron microscope (TEM) and fluorescence lifetime, were used for the analysis of physical, chemical and optical properties of Cu NCs@PEI. The Cu NCs@PEI displayed a blue-green emission wavelength at 485 nm and the corresponding quantum yield (QY) was 2.24%. Subsequently, the as-synthesized Cu NCs@PEI were applied for the trace analysis of oxytetracycline on the basis of the inner filter effect (IFE) and static quenching, which caused the fluorescence to be effectively quenched. Additionally, this proposed platform based on the Cu NCs@PEI for oxytetracycline sensing possessed an excellent linear range from 0.5 to 300 μM with a lower detection limit of 0.032 μM (S/N = 3) and correlation coefficient R2 of 0.9972. Surprisingly, as the temperature increased, the fluorescence intensities of Cu NCs@PEI decreased gradually. Thence, the Cu NCs@PEI could be applied for the sensing of temperature. Finally, the practicability of the detection method have been successfully verified by analyzing oxytetracycline in real samples, enlarging its applicable fields for oxytetracycline sensing.

head.ce:title: Highly luminescent copper nanoclusters as temperature sensors and “turn off” detection of oxytetracycline

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Polyethyleneimine (PEI), playing roles as a templating agent, has been successfully applied to prepare blue-green-emitting copper nanoclusters (Cu NCs@PEI) on the basis of a rapid chemical reduction synthesis method. Some characterization methods, such as fluorescence spectrophotometer, UV spectrophotometer, Fourier transform infrared instrument (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), transmission electron microscope (TEM) and fluorescence lifetime, were used for the analysis of physical, chemical and optical properties of Cu NCs@PEI. The Cu NCs@PEI displayed a blue-green emission wavelength at 485 nm and the corresponding quantum yield (QY) was 2.24%. Subsequently, the as-synthesized Cu NCs@PEI were applied for the trace analysis of oxytetracycline on the basis of the inner filter effect (IFE) and static quenching, which caused the fluorescence to be effectively quenched. Additionally, this proposed platform based on the Cu NCs@PEI for oxytetracycline sensing possessed an excellent linear range from 0.5 to 300 μM with a lower detection limit of 0.032 μM (S/N = 3) and correlation coefficient R2 of 0.9972. Surprisingly, as the temperature increased, the fluorescence intensities of Cu NCs@PEI decreased gradually. Thence, the Cu NCs@PEI could be applied for the sensing of temperature. Finally, the practicability of the detection method have been successfully verified by analyzing oxytetracycline in real samples, enlarging its applicable fields for oxytetracycline sensing.

ce:section.ce:para: Oxytetracycline (Fig. S1), a member of broad-spectrum antibiotics, is extracted from the culture medium of Streptomyces fissures [1,2]. As an important drug, it is widely used to treat respiratory, urethral, skin and soft tissue infections of dogs and cats due to its cheap price [3–5]. Excessive use of oxytetracycline may lead to some side reactions, such as allergic reaction, peripheral blood changes, superinfection, liver damage, nausea, vomiting, epigastric discomfort, bloating, diarrhea and so on [6,7]. Some determination methods, such as enzyme-linked immuno sorbent assay (ELISA) [8,9], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [10–13], spectrophotometry [14,15], colorimetry [16,17], liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry [18], liquid chromatography with tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [19,20], electrochemical method [21,22], fluorescent method [23–25] and Surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) [26,27], have been successfully established to determine oxytetracycline. Among these detection technologies, fluorescent determination methods have great advantages over other methods, such as simplify operation, lower cost, no professional required, and short measuring time [28–30]. However, the above analytical methods still have certain defects, such as harsh preparation conditions (high temperature and long-time), hazardous metals (rare earth quantum dots), the narrow linear range or high detection limit. Therefore, facile, practical, selective and sensitive fluorescence methods for the determination of oxytetracycline are still desired.

The core part of fluorescence analysis is the preparation of fluorescent probes. Some preparation methods, such as laser ablation [31], electrochemical method [32,33], chemical oxidation [34], hydrothermal [35,36], microwave-assisted method [37,38], ultrasound [39], template method [40], radiation reduction [41,42], chemical reduction [43,44], photoreduction [45], have been widely applied for the synthesis of fluorescent probes. Up to now, some fluorescent sensors have been developed including organic fluorescent dyes [46], metal-organic complexes [47], rare earth doped fluorescent materials [48], fluorescent proteins [49], semiconductor quantum dots [50,51], carbon quantum dots [52,53] and metal nanoclusters [54–56]. Among them, metal nanoclusters (MNCs) is comprised of several to dozens of atoms with peculiar performance limited by their sub-nanometer size [57–59]. In addition, unique features of MNCs have gained increasing interesting, strengthening it as a satisfied substitute for bioassay, bioimaging and catalysis [60]. In the past ten years, numerous reports have been carried out on fluorescent gold nanoclusters (Au NCs) and silver nanoclusters (Ag NCs) because of their fascinating characteristics, such as the large Stokes shift, ultra-small particle size distribution and excellent biocompatibility [61]. Additionally, non-noble metal copper is rich in earth, inexpensive and diffusely employed in daily life and industry. Thus, establishment of copper nanoclusters (Cu NCs) has attracted increasing attentions.

In recent years, some synthetic methods and applications of Cu NCs have been successfully developed by some researches [62,63]. For example, bovine serum albumin (BSA)-capped copper nanoclusters were synthesized and applied for the sensing of minocycline [64]. Again, Luo et al. developed a glutathione-stabilized copper nanoclusters (GSH-Cu NCs) as a fluorescent sensor for the determination of pH and vitamin B1 [65]. Similarly, our group prepared tyrosine-capped copper nanoclusters for rutin sensing. [66].

In addition, polyethyleneimine (PEI) protected Au NCs and Ag NCs have been proverbially investigated for several years because of their high quantum yield stability and strong fluorescence [67]. Moreover, these PEI capped Au NCs and Ag NCs were widely used for detecting and imaging due to their excellent chemical, physical and optical properties [68]. Interestingly, as an element of transition metal, copper has similar features with gold or silver, offering the possibility for preparing PEI-capped Cu NCs. Indeed, PEI protected Cu NCs has been successfully prepared through different synthesis methods and applied for detecting small biological molecules [69–72].

Inner filter effect (IFE) is usually considered as an error in fluorescent measurement. In recent years, IFE has gained much attention in the field of sensors, due to its simplicity, flexibility and substantially enhanced sensitivity and selectivity [73]. IFE can be caused by the absorption spectrum of the analyte overlaps with the excitation or emission spectrum of fluorophores. Designing of IFE based sensors are flexible and it does not require the surface modification of the metal nanoclusters or establishing of any covalent linking between the receptor and a fluorophore [74].

In this study, a blue-green-emissive fluorescent probe for oxytetracycline sensing was developed by a facile one-pot synthetic strategy. As displayed in Scheme 1, the blue-green-emissive fluorescent Cu NCs were synthesized through PEI as the template agent and hydrazine hydrate as the reducing agent. The as-developed Cu NCs@PEI were very stable during the long storage time. The fluorescence of Cu NCs@PEI could be quenched by oxytetracycline based on the IFE and static quenching. The main detection application were also investigated in real samples. Finally, as a temperature probe, Cu NCs@PEI exhibited a wide linear range towards temperature and could be used for multiple cycles.

The F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) and 2450 UV–visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) were employed to discuss the optical properties of Cu NCs@PEI. The Fourier transform infrared (FITR) date of Cu NCs@PEI were obtained through FTIR-8400S Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (Shimadzu Corporation, Japan) at room temperature. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) data were carried out on ESCALAB 250XI (United States). The calibration procedure and fitting procedure were performed on the XPSPEAK41. The FEI Tecnai F30 (United States) was applied for the measurement of transmission electron microscope (TEM) images of Cu NCs@PEI at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. TEM specimens were prepared by placing several drops of the Cu NCs suspension onto a carbon coated copper grid and then dried at room temperature. The fluorescence lifetime spectra of Cu NCs@PEI without and with oxytetracycline were detected by using FLS-1000 combined fluorescence lifetime and steadystate spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK). The mixture was stirred through magnetic stirrer (DF-101S, China). The pH values of the mixture were recorded by using FE20 pH-meter (Mettler Toledo Instrument Inc., Shanghai, China).

All the compounds were obtained from regular manufacturers and directly employed without any purification. Copric chloride dihydrate (CuCl2.2H2O, AR), polyethyleneimine (PEI, MW=10000, 99%), hydrazine hydrate (N2H4·H2O, 80%), sodium chloride (NaCl, 99.5%), potassium chloride (KCl, 99.5%), manganese chloride tetrahydrate (MnCl2·4H2O, 99%), magnesium chloride (MgCl2, 99%), calcium chloride dihydrate (CaCl2·2H2O, AR), aluminum chloride (AlCl3, 99%), zinc chloride (ZnCl2, ≥98%), potassium bromide (KBr, 99%), potassium iodide (KI, 99%), sodium carbonate decahydrate (Na2CO3·10H2O, 99%), sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3, ≥99.8%), sodium acetate trihydrate (NaAc, 99%), iron chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O, 99%), streptomycin (90%), artemisinin (98%), penicillin potassium (98%), lincomycin hydrochloride (95%), amtolmetin guacil (95%), hydrocortisone (98%), glycine (≥99%), alanosine (99%), glutamate (99%), cysteine (99%), oxytetracycline (≥98%), ascorbic acid (99.99%), vitamin B12 (98%), vitamin B6 (99%), human serum albumin (96%), hemoglobin were analytical grade and obtained from Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water (18.25 MΩ·cm−1) was obtained after multiple purifications by Millipore Milli-Q (PUTPLUS-15).

The Cu NCs@PEI were fabricated by using PEI as the templating agent and N2H4·H2O as the reducing agent with minor modifications [69]. In brief, CuCl2 (25 μL, 0.1 M) and PEI (50 μL, 0.01 M) were added into 445 μL Ultra-pure water at room temperature with stirring for 5 min. Afterwards, 30 μL of diluted 100 times N2H4·H2O was dropped into the above mixture at 95 °C for 12 h. The colour of the solutions varied from dark blue to light blue, which indicated that the Cu NCs@PEI were successfully prepared. Furthermore, the light blue solution was purified by using dialysis membrane (MW: 3500 Da) at room temperature for 24 h. Finally, the purified Cu NCs@PEI were saved in the refrigerator for further use.

For the sensing of oxytetracycline, a series of Cu NCs@PEI (1.0 mL) and PBS (1.0 mL, pH=7.0) mixed solutions were obtained in 5.0 mL colorimetric tube. Then, different concentrations of oxytetracycline were added into the above colorimetric tube, respectively. The mixed solutions were thoroughly reacted at room temperature for 90 s before the measuring of the fluorescence intensities. The corresponding fluorescence data were obtained at the excitation wavelength of 410 nm.

For the selective experiment of Cu NCs@PEI towards oxytetracycline, different controls were added into the Cu NCs@PEI and PBS mixture, including Na+, K+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Br-, I-, CO3 2-, HCO3 -, Ac-, Fe3+, Cu2+, streptomycin, artemisinin, penicillin potassium, lincomycin hydrochloride, amtolmetin guacil, hydrocortisone, glycine, alanosine, glutamate, cysteine, ascorbic acid, vitamin B12, vitamin B6, human serum albumin, hemoglobin (the concentration was 300 μM). The fluorescent spectra and intensities were recorded under the same conditions.

The bovine serum samples were obtained from Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co. Ltd. The human serum samples were obtained from school clinic. The Mengniu milk samples were purchased from the supermarket. Many pretreatments were implemented through a series of steps. First of all, the real samples were centrifuged for the removal of larger particle impurities and diluted 100 times through the PBS solution (pH=7.0). Then, different oxytetracycline standard real samples were obtained through multiple dilutions. Finally, the FL intensities of the samples with oxytetracycline were recorded. The measured concentrations were calculated by the linear equation. Meanwhile, the recoveries (%) were calculated based on the Eq. (1). The relative standard deviation (RSD) was performed through repeating the test three times under the same conditions.

The quantum yield (QY) of the Cu NCs@PEI was obtained based on the reported methods [75]. All data were obtained at the excitation wavelength of 410 nm and slit band widths of 10/10 nm. The calculation equation was as follows (2).

where QY is the quantum yield of Cu NCs@PEI, I represents the integral area under fluorescent spectra, η refers to the refractive index of solvent, A is the UV–vis absorbance at the excitation wavelength of 410 nm. R is the reference.

Some properties of Cu NCs@PEI were determined through the TEM, XPS, FTIR, UV–vis absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. As displayed in Fig. 1A, the TEM image indicated that the Cu NCs@PEI possessed a highly dispersed spherical shape. By counting the diameters of 100 particles, the average size of Cu NCs@PEI could reach 2.1 ± 0.032 nm (Fig. 1B). The fluorescence spectra of PEI, CuCl2, N2H4·H2O and the as-developed Cu NCs@PEI were shown in Fig. 2A. Compared with PEI, CuCl2 and N2H4·H2O, a new emission peak at 485 nm of Cu NCs@PEI was discovered, which indicated that the synthesis of Cu NCs@PEI was successful [76]. The maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of Cu NCs@PEI were 410 and 485 nm, respectively (Fig. 2B). The QY of the Cu NCs@PEI was measured as 2.44% according to the Eq. (2). In addition, some methods, such as XPS and FTIR, were used for the study of the surface active functional groups of the Cu NCs@PEI. As depicted in Fig. 3A, there were four peaks at 284.36, 398.13, 529.91 and 932.79 eV in the full spectrum of XPS. The four peaks corresponded to C1s, N1s, O1s and Cu2p [77]. Furthermore, the peaks of Cu2p1/2 and Cu2p3/2 at 951.12 and 931.20 eV were ascribed to Cu0 or Cu+. The peaks of Cu2p1/2 and Cu2p3/2 at 953.47 and 933.63 eV might be existence of Cu-N bond (Fig. 3B). Additionally, the FTIR measurement of Cu NCs@PEI was also carried out. Fig. S2 displayed that stretching vibration absorption peak of -NH was located at 3416 cm−1. The stretching vibration peak at 1125 cm−1 referred to C-N bond, and the peak at 1617 cm−1 was assumed to in-plane bending vibration of -NH bond. The above test results suggested that some active functional groups existed on the surface of Cu NCs@PEI, which was the main reason for excellent water solubility of Cu NCs@PEI.

The as-synthesized Cu NCs@PEI had excellent stability. When Cu NCs@PEI were stored for 15 days, it still maintained strong fluorescence (Fig. S3A). Furthermore, the influences of UV irradiation time on the fluorescence of Cu NCs@PEI were discussed. As displayed in Fig. S3B, after 15 min of UV exposure, the fluorescence quenching degree of Cu NCs@PEI was only 2.12%, indicating that Cu NCs@PEI had excellent light resistance. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensities of Cu NCs@PEI did not decrease even the NaCl concentration reached 0.25 M (Fig. S3C). The effects of different solvents on the stability of Cu NCs@PEI were studied such as water, PBS solution (6.0, 6.5, 7.0, 7.5 and 8.0) and bovine serum. As displayed in Fig. S3D, the fluorescence intensities of Cu NCs@PEI decreased with the pH value increasing from 6.0 to 8.0. When the Cu NCs@PEI and water or bovine serum were mixed, its intensity changed indistinctively.

To optimize the determination sensitivity of Cu NCs@PEI towards oxytetracycline, the influences of pH values on the sensing of oxytetracycline were first discussed. As displayed in Fig. S4A, the fluorescence response value reached the maximum for oxytetracycline under the pH value of 7.0. Then, the fluorescence intensities of the sensing system with oxytetracycline and without oxytetracycline were recorded under different reaction times. The best detection condition was 90 s (Fig. S4B). The detection of oxytetracycline was carried out under optimal detection conditions. Different amounts of oxytetracycline were dropped into the detection system. With the increasing of oxytetracycline amounts (from 0 to 300 μM), the FL intensities of determination system decreased ( Fig. 4A). As shown in Fig. 4B, a linear fitting curve was obtained between ln(F
0
/F) and concentrations of oxytetracycline (from 0.5 to 300 μM). The corresponding linear regression formula was ln(F
0
/F) = 0.0033[Q]−0.0062 (R2 = 0.9972), where [Q] represents the amounts of oxytetracycline. F 0 and F refers to the FL intensities of Cu NCs@PEI without and with oxytetracycline, respectively. According to the S/N = 3, the limit of detection (LOD) could reach 0.032 μM. Compared with other methods, the linear range of this platform was wider than others ( Table 1). Therefore, the developed detection system provided great feasibility for the analysis of oxytetracycline.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity of the Cu NCs@PEI for oxytetracycline
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the specificity of the as-developed Cu NCs@PEI, some control substances were studied such as Na+, K+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Br-, I-, CO3 2-, HCO3 -, Ac-, Fe3+, Cu2+, streptomycin, artemisinin, penicillin potassium, lincomycin hydrochloride, amtolmetin guacil, hydrocortisone, glycine, alanosine, glutamate, cysteine, ascorbic acid, vitamin B12, vitamin B6, human serum albumin, hemoglobin. The fluorescence intensities of Cu NCs@PEI with (F) and without (F 0) control substances at 485 nm were recorded. The fluorescence quenching degree could be evaluated by the F 0/F value, and the higher F 0/F value intimated huge fluorescence quenching rate. The F 0/F values of all control substances were displayed in Fig. 5. Surprisingly, the F 0/F values of control substances lied between 0.9 and 1.2, while the value of oxytetracycline was much greater than 1. Competitive experiments were also conducted to study the influences of other references. The quenching caused by mixing oxytetracycline with the other references was observed (Fig. S5A and Fig. S5B). The excellent selectivity of Cu NCs@PEI towards oxytetracycline provided great advantages for its applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching mechanism of the Cu NCs@PEI by oxytetracycline
ce:section.ce:para: Some studies have been performed to discuss the fluorescence quenching mechanisms of the Cu NCs@PEI towards oxytetracycline. The state of copper was investigated with and without oxytetracycline. It could be seen from Fig. 6A, the valence of copper of Cu NCs@PEI was still zero or one, indicating that the reason for the fluorescence quenchings of Cu NCs@PEI were not the change of the valence state of copper.

The mechanisms of fluorescence quenching were continuously studied by UV–vis spectra and fluorescence lifetime. As displayed in Fig. 6B, an absorption peak of oxytetracycline at 363 nm was observed, which obviously overlapped with the fluorescent excitation peak of Cu NCs@PEI. This overlapping phenomenon indicated that the quenching mechanism may be attributed to the IFE or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [78]. Furthermore, the IFE and FRET could be distinguished by fluorescence lifetime. The fluorescence lifetime spectra of Cu NCs@PEI with and without oxytetracycline were displayed in Fig. 6C. The average fluorescence lifetime could be calculated by using Eq. (3). The average fluorescence lifetime of Cu NCs@PEI was 4.95 ns, the time constant was 1.84 ns (29.76%) and 6.26 ns (70.24%). The average fluorescence lifetime of Cu NCs@PEI+oxytetracycline solution was 4.59 ns, the time constant was 1.57 ns (36.44%) and 5.79 ns (63.56%). Before and after adding oxytetracycline, the fluorescence lifetime of Cu NCs@PEI had almost no change, which excluded the existence of FRET [79].

Among them, the values of F obsd were recorded through fluorescence spectroscopy and the values of F cor were calculated based on Eq. (4). The suppressed efficiency of the observed (E obsd) and corrected (E cor) were listed in Table 3. The absorbance of the Cu NCs@PEI with oxytetracycline are measured (Fig. S6). The suppressed efficiency of the observed was higher than the corrected (Fig. 6D), which suggested that the main mechanism was assumed to IFE [80]. Furthermore, the fluorescence lifetime results confirmed that the fluorescence quenching mechanism was also attributed to static quenching. The static quenching was discussed through Eq. (5), which F 0 and F refers to the FL intensities of Cu NCs@PEI without and with oxytetracycline, respectively. The meaning of K SV and [Q] were quenching constant and oxytetracycline concentration. τ 0 refers to the fluorescent lifetime (4.95 ns). K SV value was equal to 1.6 × 103 L·mol−1 through the corresponding calculation (Fig. 6E). The value of Kq (3.23 × 1011 L·mol−1·s−1) was greater than the maximum scatter collision quenching constant (2 ×1010 L·mol−1·s−1), which confirmed the existence of the static quenching. Finally, a new peak was observed in the UV spectrum of Cu NCs@PEI+oxytetracycline, indicating that the interaction between Cu NCs@PEI and oxytetracycline was observed (Fig. 6F). The static quenching was further confirmed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of oxytetracycline in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To appraise the practical application ability of this system for oxytetracycline analysis, the practical experiments of oxytetracycline determination in bovine serum sample, human serum sample and Mengniu milk sample were first studied. Since oxytetracycline could be not determined in samples, the standard addition recovery methods were carried out. As displayed in Table 4, the real sample recoveries were located at 94.4%−108.8%. Meanwhile, the relative standard deviation (RSD) of 2.34–3.89% was obtained. In this study, oxytetracycline was also quantified by a HPLC analysis method, and the findings were compared with sensor results. The recovery percentages for HPLC were found to vary from 95.6% to 107.8%. The RSD was lower than 3.47%. The effective recovery of the oxytetracycline demonstrated that the probe had promising application for determination of oxytetracycline in real samples.

In order to develop the applicability of the as-prepared Cu NCs@PEI for the determination of temperature, the temperature detection of Cu NCs@PEI was discussed. The fluorescence spectra of Cu NCs@PEI under different temperature (15 °C to 85 °C) were recorded by fluorescence spectrophotometer. As displayed in Fig. 7A, when the temperature rose from 15 °C to 85 °C, the peak height of the fluorescence spectrum of Cu NCs@PEI at 485 nm gradually decreased. The above phenomenon showed that high temperature possessed fluorescence quenching effect for the Cu NCs@PEI. The fluorescence quenching effect was ascribed to the increase of molecular collision frequency and nonradiative transition rate. However, the radiative transition rate was basically unchanged under high temperature. Then, the emission intensity of excited state would decrease [81]. A linear relationship was obtained between FL intensity and temperature changing from 15 °C to 55 °C. The linear regression formula was F = −12.73[Tem] + 1300 (R2 = 0.9889) (Fig. 7B). Surprisingly, the fluorescence of Cu NCs@PEI could gradually recover with the temperature dropping to 15 °C. Finally, the Cu NCs@PEI possessed reusability for temperature detection (Fig. 7C).

In the study, blue-green emissive fluorescence copper nanoclusters was successfully fabricated by a chemical reduction method. The as-established Cu NCs@PEI possessed the QY of 2.24%, narrow particle size distribution range and good stability. Oxytetracycline could obviously quench the fluorescence of Cu NCs@PEI. The quenching mechanisms were ascribed to the IFE and static quenching. Based on this feature, a fluorescent method for oxytetracycline detection was established. Under the best detection conditions, a wider linear range was obtained between 0.5 and 300 μM, and the LOD could reach 0.032 μM. Meanwhile, the Cu NCs@PEI had a fluorescence response effect on temperature. Finally, this platform was successfully applied to detect oxytetracycline in real samples.

Zhi-feng Cai: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Hao-yang Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Xian-song Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Can Min: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Jia-qi Wen, Rui-xin Fu, Zi-yue Dai: measure fluorescence. Ming-zhu Guo, Hui-juan Yang: measure UV–visible spectra. Ping-ping Bai, Ying Wu: performing the experiments. Jing Chen, Tao Wu: grammar correction.
